retrieved_docs.title: Ankle Fracture -- Treatment / Management -- Open reduction and internal fixation
retrieved_docs.content: Posterior malleolus fractures can be addressed with a posteroanterior lag screw or an antiglide plate. Intraoperative assessment of the ankle syndesmosis is essential and can be performed using the Hook test (Cotton test) or intraoperative stress external rotation views to confirm proper alignment and stability. [20] [21]  The Hook test can be performed by the lateral distraction of the fibula with a bone hook under the image intensifier screen. The Hook test is considered positive if there is more than 2 mm of displacement of the fibula. [22]  If the hook test is positive and there is a syndesmosis diastasis, the syndesmosis should be reduced by applying a big reduction clamp to the medial and lateral malleoli and then fixed either by syndesmotic screws or tightropes.

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Ring Injuries -- Treatment / Management -- Open reduction internal fixation (ORIF):
retrieved_docs.content: ORIF results in improved quality of reduction and better biomechanical stability compared to external fixation. Plates and screws across the symphysis can support the anterior ring. [22] Parasymphyseal pubic ramus fracture is treatable with open plating or percutaneous screw fixation. Sacroilial dislocation or sacral fracture can be treated with sacroiliac, transiliac transacral, or transiliac screws percutaneously. [23] A thorough understanding of pelvic ring osseous fixation pathways is necessary to perform these techniques safely. Sacral dysmorphism is common and can complicate safe percutaneous fixation of the posterior pelvic ring. [24] Open plating of sacroiliac joints or sacral fractures may be necessary if a closed reduction is unobtainable or the fracture pattern is not amenable to percutaneous fixation.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Bone Immobilization -- Issues of Concern -- Types of Splints
retrieved_docs.content: Pelvic circumferential compression devices (pelvic binder) work to provide circumferential stabilization of the entire pelvic cavity. This is performed to reduce pelvic fractures and decrease pelvic volume, in turn leading to decreased hemorrhage. The compression force is applied at the level of the femoral trochanters. This can also be achieved by wrapping a sheet around the pelvis and tying it tightly to secure it in place.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bony Pelvis and Lower Limb: Femur -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Nondisplaced fractures are treated with cancellous lag screws or a sliding hip screw. Both techniques have about the same outcomes and complications. Cancellous lag screws are associated with shorter operative time and less blood loss. However, cancellous lag screws have a higher revision rate than sliding hip screws due to prominent lag screw head irritation of soft tissue as the naturally femoral neck shortens postoperatively. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Forearm Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Limited Contact Dynamic Compression Plate
retrieved_docs.content: Perren et al. reported the use of limited contact dynamic compression plate (LCDCP) in 1990. The fixation principle of LCDCP is very similar to the DCP. The advantages over the DCP are 1) decreased periosteal compression and damage, 2) preserves blood supply to the bone, 3) wavy surface provides an increased area for bone graft application, 4) altering the load of the plate delivers pure tension on the plate, and 5) avoids implant-related stress riser and thereby re-fracture after implant removal. [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications and contraindications of external fixation. Describe the equipment, biomechanics, and technique in regards to external fixation. Review appropriate evaluation of the potential complications and clinical significance of external fixation. Explain interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance external fixation treatment and improve outcomes. retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Management of Femoral Neck Fractures -- Technique or Treatment -- Sliding Hip Screw
retrieved_docs.content: The lag screw is then applied over the wire and screwed in using the attached handle. The handle's final position should be parallel to the femur to ensure the lag screw will slot into the plating barrel. The plate is then slid over the lag screw. The plate template may be needed to ensure the plate is fully advanced. The guidewire can now be removed. The plate may be secured to the femur using a clamp.

retrieved_docs.title: Talar Neck Fractures -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A dual-incision technique involving anteromedial and anterolateral approaches is commonly used to ensure accurate fracture reduction. An alternative sinus tarsi approach can be utilized as a lateral window, with a slightly more plantar incision from the distal fibula toward the 4th metatarsal. This approach provides an easier visualization of the lateral process. Exposures can be augmented with malleolar osteotomies to aid in visualizing the talar neck and body. Soft tissue considerations, fracture type, and other associated injuries often dictate the exact surgical approach. There are various reports of screw, plate, and hybrid fixation strategies. An estimated 96% of reported talar neck fractures were addressed with some combination of plate and screw constructs. [13] Longitudinal lag screws, especially on the medial side, are typically contraindicated because too much compression through medial comminution may result in a varus collapse or deformity. Published literature comparing screw constructs versus plate and screw constructs did not find one method superior to the other in terms of maintaining fixation and providing biomechanical stability. [11] Results from a study evaluated and compared screws alone versus screw and blade plate fixation, and the authors found no significant differences in yield point, stiffness, or load to create a 3 mm deformation. [12] This study utilized conventional 3.5 mm cortical or 4.0 mm cancellous screws. However, the authors suggest that headless variable-pitch screws would improve fixation strength compared to the conventional cannulated screws used in their investigation.

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Management of Femoral Neck Fractures -- Indications -- Cannulated Screw Fixation
retrieved_docs.content: Critical utilization of partially threaded (long or short thread options) screw options should be preoperatively planned before the procedure begins. The surgeon should plan on optimizing the "lag by design" technique. Recently, a femoral neck system implant has been described for the internal fixation of FNFs in young patients with theoretical biomechanical advantages compared to other implants. [23] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: External fixation has been used for the treatment of fractures for 2000 years. The application and devises have changed, but the biomechanics remain the same. This activity outlines the relevant anatomy, techniques, and fixation goals and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients who undergo external fixation.

retrieved_docs.title: Lisfranc Dislocation -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Management Of Lisfranc Injuries
retrieved_docs.content: ORIF may involve either of the following techniques: Transarticular-screw placement: This traditional cheap option avoids extensive soft-tissue stripping. However, a single 3.5-mm transarticular screw has been shown to damage articular surfaces by 2 to 3.6% on average. [19] [20] Dorsal-bridge plating: This technique provides superior functional outcomes compared to transarticular-screw placement. Dorsal bridge plating is useful in repairing highly comminuted injuries. Suture button: This newer technique has promising results but needs more evidence.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bony Pelvis and Lower Limb: Metatarsal Bones -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Lisfranc injuries are characterized by the disruption of the articulation between the medial cuneiform and the base of the second metatarsal, a tarsometatarsal fracture-dislocation, disrupting the TMT joint complex. This type of injury most commonly occurs as the result of high energy trauma in the setting of a motor vehicle accident, fall from height, or athletic injury due to an axial load on a hyper-plantar flexed forefoot. Lisfranc injuries can be managed nonoperatively through cast immobilization when there is no displacement on weight-bearing or stress radiographs or no evidence of bony injuries on CT. Often, Lisfranc injuries are treated operatively with open reduction internal fixation or, more commonly, primary arthrodesis of involved metatarsal joints. Recent studies indicate primary arthrodesis results in decreased rates of revision surgery or hardware removal with equivalent functional outcomes. [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: External Fixation Principles and Overview -- Introduction -- Influential Factors and Variables for Construct Stiffness and Stability
retrieved_docs.content: One method of changing the stability is to alter the pin configuration. Placing pins closer to the fracture site, adding more pins and increasing the spread of the pins will all add to the stiffness of the construct. However, one must also place the pins out of the field of future surgical approaches during definitive fixation. Any increases in pin diameter will strengthen the construct to the fourth power and reduce the stress at the bone-pin interface. Increasing pin diameter has the greatest influence on the stability of unilateral frames. That said, larger pins increase the risk of a potential stress riser and can ultimately lead to fracture. [1] For example; a 5 mm pin is 144% stiffer than a 4 mm pin.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bony Pelvis and Lower Limb: Thigh Sartorius Muscle -- Clinical Significance -- Anterior Superior Iliac Spine (ASIS) Avulsion
retrieved_docs.content: Open reduction internal fixation (ORIF) is indicated when the avulsion of the ASIS displaces by a distance of more than 2 or 3 centimeters. Painful non-unions are also an indication for surgical fixation. There are several different options, including lag screw and tension band fixation. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Management of Femoral Neck Fractures -- Indications -- Sliding Hip Screw Fixation
retrieved_docs.content: The fracture can be compressed as weight-bearing forces transfer from the appendicular skeleton through the pelvis and femoral head and neck before propagating distally. Thus, the SHS fixation construct allows for longitudinal sliding and compression as the patient ambulates postoperatively. The fracture compresses down as the lag screw slides into the barrel and further promotes healing. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 823
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)) is a condition marked by diminished bone density that predisposes patients to skeletal fragility. Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر), hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ), and the occurrence of multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are all recognized as clinical entities that belong to the osteoporosis/low bone mass category. These procedures and fracture patterns commonly arise because weakened bone is less able to withstand normal stresses, necessitating surgical stabilization or joint replacement. Effective management of osteoporosis/low bone mass therefore aims to strengthen bone, reduce the incidence of such fractures, and limit the need for invasive orthopedic interventions.

retrieved_docs.content: crash, is associated with extensive tissue damage, reinforcing the link between high‑energy fractures, tissue injury, and neurotoxic effects. Overall, fracture (كسر) integrates late sequelae, specific anatomical fracture types, associated tissue damage, co‑occurring injuries, procedural interventions, and psychosocial classifications, providing a comprehensive framework for patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is classified as a treatment modality for several specific orthopedic injuries. It is considered a type of treatment for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, for fractures or dislocations of the lower extremity excluding the hip and femur, and for fractures or dislocations of the radius and ulna. In each of these contexts, the procedure involves surgically stabilizing the bone without performing a reduction of the fracture, thereby providing a consistent therapeutic approach across multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 771
retrieved_docs.content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is classified as a treatment for both fracture or dislocation of the hip and femur and for fracture or dislocation of the lower extremity other than the hip or femur. This procedure is closely related to the Arabic‑language concept تثبيت داخلي للكسر (internal fixation of fracture), which is considered the inverse of the English term, indicating that they represent reciprocal perspectives on the same surgical approach. Moreover, Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is a specific form of Internal fixation of clavicle without fracture reduction, highlighting its applicability to clavicular injuries when reduction is not performed. Both تثبيت داخلي للكسر and إعاضة رأس الفخذ (hip head replacement) are listed as members of the SMQ category هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (osteoporosis/low bone mass), underscoring the clinical relevance of these surgical interventions in patients with compromised bone quality. Together, these relationships illustrate how internal fixation procedures, including those without fracture reduction, fit within broader treatment categories for lower‑extremity injuries and are particularly pertinent to the management of osteoporotic bone conditions.

retrieved_docs.content: Crushing injuries or internal injuries are associated with open wounds (جرح مفتوح), indicating that such traumatic forces often result in visible tissue disruption.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 261
retrieved_docs.content: and “كسور متعددة” (multiple fractures) illustrate the spectrum of interventions and clinical manifestations that arise in patients with compromised bone health. By connecting specific fracture entities, a comprehensive set of internal‑fixation and open‑reduction techniques, and hierarchical procedural categories to underlying bone‑density disorders, the knowledge base supports integrated treatment planning for orthopedic injuries of the hip, lower extremity, and shoulder‑arm region, while highlighting the importance of managing osteoporosis to reduce fracture risk and optimize surgical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 950
retrieved_docs.content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is a specific surgical treatment used for managing fractures or dislocations of several long bones, including the hip and femur as well as the radius and ulna. It is classified as a particular form of internal fixation of a fracture (تثبيت داخلي للكسر), indicating that it belongs to the broader category of internal fixation procedures performed without prior fracture reduction. Additionally, this procedure is considered a subtype of internal fixation techniques applied to the clavicle without fracture reduction, reflecting its inclusion within the general family of bone‑specific internal fixation methods. Together, these relationships highlight that internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is a targeted operative approach that fits within larger treatment categories for various skeletal injuries, emphasizing its role in orthopedic care for multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Osteoporosis/low bone mass (SMQ) includes several fracture‑related conditions and orthopedic interventions. Internal fixation of fractures (تثبيت داخلي للكسر) is a member of the osteoporosis/low bone mass category, reflecting that patients with reduced bone density often require surgical stabilization of broken bones. Hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) also belongs to this category, indicating that severe osteoporotic changes can lead to hip joint damage that necessitates prosthetic replacement. In addition, other fractures are listed as members of osteoporosis/low bone mass, underscoring the broad susceptibility of osteoporotic individuals to multiple fracture types. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians anticipate and manage orthopedic needs in patients with osteoporosis or low bone mass.
